text.content:
Welcome, Acolyte

Dark Heresy 2nd Edition is a roleplaying game set in the grim darkness of the Warhammer 40,000 universe. In the 41st Millennium, humanity is beset on all sides by aliens, heretics, and daemons. The Imperium of Man stretches across a million worlds, held together by faith, sacrifice, and the iron will of the God-Emperor who sits upon the Golden Throne of Terra.

You play as Acolytes — agents of the Imperial Inquisition, humanity's secret protectors. Recruited from all walks of Imperial life, Acolytes serve an Inquisitor, investigating threats that would destroy the Imperium from within: heresy, mutation, xenos infiltration, and the corrupting influence of the Warp.

The Inquisition

The Inquisition operates above Imperial law. An Inquisitor's authority is near-absolute, backed by the Seal of the Inquisition. Acolytes carry out their master's will — investigating leads, interrogating suspects, purging threats, and uncovering conspiracies that lesser authorities cannot or will not confront.

The work is dangerous. The enemies are cunning. Trust is a luxury. But the Imperium endures because men and women like you stand between it and annihilation.

Character Creation Overview

Creating a character in Dark Heresy 2nd Edition follows six steps:

  1. Generate Characteristics — Roll your nine core attributes.
  2. Choose a Homeworld — Where you were born shapes who you are.
  3. Choose a Background — Your prior life and training before the Inquisition recruited you.
  4. Choose a Role — Your function within the warband.
  5. Spend Experience Points — Customise your character with 1,000 starting XP.
  6. Select Equipment — Gear up and calculate your derived stats.

Each step builds on the last. Your homeworld, background, and role together determine your starting skills, talents, aptitudes, and equipment. Take your time with each decision — your choices define not just what your character can do, but who they are.

Generating Characteristics

Dark Heresy 2nd Edition uses nine characteristics that define your character's physical and mental capabilities. Each characteristic is rated on a scale, typically ranging from the low 20s to the mid 40s for starting characters.

The Nine Characteristics

  • Weapon Skill (WS) — Proficiency in melee combat.
  • Ballistic Skill (BS) — Proficiency with ranged weapons.
  • Strength (S) — Physical power and muscle.
  • Toughness (T) — Endurance, resilience, and physical durability.
  • Agility (Ag) — Speed, reflexes, and coordination.
  • Intelligence (Int) — Reasoning, memory, and analytical ability.
  • Perception (Per) — Awareness and acuity of senses.
  • Willpower (WP) — Mental fortitude, discipline, and resistance to psychic influence.
  • Fellowship (Fel) — Charisma, social ability, and force of personality.

Rolling Characteristics

For each characteristic, roll 2d10 + 20. This gives a range of 22–40 before homeworld modifiers are applied.

Note: Dark Heresy 2nd Edition uses 2d10+20, not 2d10+25 as in Rogue Trader. This lower baseline reflects that Acolytes are ordinary humans thrust into extraordinary circumstances, not the powerful figures of a Rogue Trader dynasty.

Rearranging Characteristics

After rolling all nine characteristics, you may swap any two characteristic values — but only one pair. Choose carefully. This is your one opportunity to move a high roll where you need it most.

Characteristic Bonuses

Each characteristic has an associated Characteristic Bonus, equal to the tens digit of the characteristic value. For example:

  • A Strength of 34 gives a Strength Bonus of 3.
  • A Toughness of 41 gives a Toughness Bonus of 4.

Characteristic Bonuses are used throughout the game for derived stats (wounds, movement, carrying capacity) and various mechanical effects. A bonus of 3 versus 4 can be the difference between life and death.

Choose Your Homeworld

Your homeworld is where you were born and raised. It shapes your character's outlook, physicality, and starting capabilities. Each homeworld modifies your characteristics, determines your starting wounds and fate threshold, and grants unique skills, talents, and aptitudes.

Feral World

Primitive, brutal planets where survival demands constant struggle against the environment and other predators.

  • Characteristic Modifiers: +Strength, +Toughness, −Influence
  • Wounds: 9 + 1d5
  • Fate Threshold: 2 (Emperor's Blessing: 3)
  • Starting Skills: Survival
  • Starting Talent: Iron Jaw or Frenzy
  • Aptitude: Toughness
  • Homeworld Bonus — The Old Ways: A feral world character treats the primitive quality of weapons as having no effect, and never suffers penalties for being unfamiliar with Low-Tech or primitive environments.

Forge World

Industrial worlds dominated by the Adeptus Mechanicus, where the Omnissiah is revered and technology permeates every aspect of life.

  • Characteristic Modifiers: +Intelligence, +Toughness, −Fellowship
  • Wounds: 8 + 1d5
  • Fate Threshold: 3 (Emperor's Blessing: 8)
  • Starting Skills: Tech-Use or Logic
  • Starting Talent: Weapon Training (Solid Projectile) or Mechadendrite Use (Utility)
  • Aptitude: Intelligence
  • Homeworld Bonus — Omnissiah's Chosen: A forge world character counts as having the Technical Knock talent and gains a +10 bonus to Tech-Use tests to repair or maintain items.

Highborn

Scions of noble houses, raised in privilege and educated in the arts of leadership, politics, and command.

  • Characteristic Modifiers: +Fellowship, +Influence, −Toughness
  • Wounds: 9 + 1d5
  • Fate Threshold: 4 (Emperor's Blessing: 10)
  • Starting Skills: Linguistics (High Gothic) or Lore (any one)
  • Starting Talent: Peer (Nobility) or Peer (any one)
  • Aptitude: Fellowship
  • Homeworld Bonus — Breeding Counts: A highborn character reduces the Influence cost to acquire Rare or Very Rare items by 1, and may spend an Influence point to gain a +10 bonus on any social Interaction test.

Hive World

Vast city-planets with populations in the billions, where life is cheap and the strong prey on the weak in the underhive depths.

  • Characteristic Modifiers: +Agility, +Perception, −Willpower
  • Wounds: 8 + 1d5
  • Fate Threshold: 2 (Emperor's Blessing: 6)
  • Starting Skills: Deceive or Logic
  • Starting Talent: Weapon Training (Solid Projectile) or Weapon Training (Chain)
  • Aptitude: Perception
  • Homeworld Bonus — Teeming Masses in Metal Mountains: A hive world character ignores crowds for the purpose of movement and never takes penalties for operating in enclosed or urbanised spaces. They also gain a +20 bonus to Navigate (Surface) tests in hive or urban environments.

Shrine World

Worlds dedicated entirely to the worship of the God-Emperor, where faith is a living force and devotion shapes every moment of existence.

  • Characteristic Modifiers: +Willpower, +Fellowship, −Perception
  • Wounds: 8 + 1d5
  • Fate Threshold: 3 (Emperor's Blessing: 6)
  • Starting Skills: Lore (Theology)
  • Starting Talent: Pure Faith or Flagellant
  • Aptitude: Willpower
  • Homeworld Bonus — Faith in the Creed: A shrine world character gains a +10 bonus to any one test per session when invoking the name of the Emperor or a recognised saint.

Voidborn

Born aboard starships or space stations, voidborn are touched by the strangeness of the Warp and marked as outsiders on solid ground.

  • Characteristic Modifiers: +Intelligence, +Willpower, −Strength
  • Wounds: 7 + 1d5
  • Fate Threshold: 3 (Emperor's Blessing: 5)
  • Starting Skills: Navigate (Stellar) or Pilot (Spacecraft)
  • Starting Talent: Strong Minded or Resistance (Fear)
  • Aptitude: Intelligence
  • Homeworld Bonus — Child of the Dark: A voidborn character gains a +30 bonus to tests for moving in zero-gravity environments and never suffers the effects of space travel sickness. They also gain the Resistance (Cold) talent.

Choose Your Background

Your background represents your character's life before they were recruited by the Inquisition. It determines additional starting skills, talents, aptitudes, and equipment. Your background also establishes which Imperial institution or walk of life shaped your training and worldview.

Adeptus Administratum

The vast bureaucracy that manages the Imperium. Clerks, scribes, logisticians, and functionaries who know how the machinery of government actually works.

  • Starting Skills: Commerce, Linguistics (High Gothic), Logic
  • Starting Talent: Weapon Training (Las or Solid Projectile)
  • Aptitude: Knowledge
  • Starting Equipment: Laspistol or stub automatic, Imperial robes, auto-quill, chrono, dataslate
  • Background Bonus: +10 to Requisition tests when acquiring items through official channels.

Adeptus Arbites

The enforcers of Imperial law. Judges, investigators, and executioners who pursue criminals and maintain order through unyielding force.

  • Starting Skills: Awareness, Intimidate, Scrutiny
  • Starting Talent: Weapon Training (Shock or Solid Projectile)
  • Aptitude: Offence
  • Starting Equipment: Shotgun or shock maul, enforcer light carapace armour, magnacles, 12 rounds
  • Background Bonus: +10 to Intimidate and Interrogation tests against Imperial citizens.

Adeptus Astra Telepathica

The organisation that trains and sanctions the Imperium's psykers. Characters from this background have been touched by the Warp and survived the soul-binding trials — or serve as the handlers who keep psykers in check.

  • Starting Skills: Awareness, Psyniscience, Scrutiny
  • Starting Talent: Weapon Training (Las or Solid Projectile), and one Sanctioned Psyker power or Resistance (Psychic Powers)
  • Aptitude: Defence
  • Starting Equipment: Laspistol, staff or whip, light flak cloak, micro-bead, psy-focus
  • Background Bonus: Characters with the Psyker elite advance may purchase psychic powers at a reduced XP cost.

Adeptus Mechanicus

The Cult of the Machine God. Tech-priests, enginseers, and acolytes of the Omnissiah who see the divine in gears, data-streams, and sacred machinery.

  • Starting Skills: Logic, Security, Tech-Use
  • Starting Talent: Mechadendrite Use (Utility) or Weapon Training (Solid Projectile)
  • Aptitude: Knowledge
  • Starting Equipment: Stub automatic or laspistol, one mechadendrite (utility), Imperial robes, combi-tool, dataslate
  • Background Bonus: +10 to Tech-Use tests. May replace one cybernetic with a superior version during character creation (GM approval).

Adeptus Ministorum

The Ecclesiarchy — the Imperial Church. Preachers, confessors, missionaries, and militant zealots who spread the Emperor's word and root out spiritual corruption.

  • Starting Skills: Charm, Command, Linguistics (High Gothic)
  • Starting Talent: Weapon Training (Flame or Las)
  • Aptitude: Social
  • Starting Equipment: Hand flamer or laspistol, chainblade or staff, Imperial robes (Good quality), backpack, micro-bead
  • Background Bonus: +10 to Charm and Command tests when appealing to the faith of Imperial citizens.

Imperial Guard

The Astra Militarum — the Hammer of the Emperor. Soldiers who have served in the endless wars that defend the Imperium's borders. Veterans of the front lines.

  • Starting Skills: Athletics, Command, Navigate (Surface)
  • Starting Talent: Weapon Training (Las or Solid Projectile or Low-Tech)
  • Aptitude: Fieldcraft
  • Starting Equipment: Lasgun or laspistol and sword, flak armour, one frag grenade, one smoke grenade, micro-bead
  • Background Bonus: +10 to Command tests in combat situations. Counts as having the Rapid Reload talent when using weapons with the Proven quality.

Outcast

Those who exist outside the Imperium's structures — criminals, scavengers, mutants, heretics-turned-informant, and those who fell through the cracks. Resourceful, cunning, and utterly expendable.

  • Starting Skills: Acrobatics or Sleight of Hand, Deceive, Stealth
  • Starting Talent: Weapon Training (Chain or Las or Solid Projectile)
  • Aptitude: Fieldcraft
  • Starting Equipment: Autopistol or laspistol, chainsword or stub revolver, armoured bodyglove, injector, two doses of obscura or stimm
  • Background Bonus: +10 to Stealth and Survival tests in urban environments. May re-roll one failed Influence test per session when dealing with the criminal underworld.

Choose Your Role

Your role defines your function within the Inquisitorial warband. While homeworld and background establish where you came from, your role determines what you do now. Each role grants aptitudes that shape your advancement costs, and a special role bonus or talent.

Assassin

Killers, snipers, and silent executioners. The Assassin eliminates targets with lethal precision.

  • Aptitudes: Agility, Ballistic Skill or Weapon Skill, Fieldcraft, Finesse, Perception
  • Role Bonus — Sure Kill: After making a successful attack, the Assassin may spend a Fate point to inflict additional damage equal to their Perception bonus on the first hit of that attack. This additional damage is not reduced by armour or Toughness bonus.

Chirurgeon

Field medics, combat surgeons, and students of the biological sciences. The Chirurgeon keeps the warband alive.

  • Aptitudes: Fieldcraft, Intelligence, Knowledge, Perception, Toughness
  • Role Bonus — Dedicated Healer: When the Chirurgeon successfully uses the Medicae skill on a patient, they heal additional wounds equal to their Intelligence bonus. The Chirurgeon also gains the Resistance (Poisons) talent.

Desperado

Gunslingers, duelists, and reckless risk-takers. The Desperado thrives in the chaos of close-quarters firefights.

  • Aptitudes: Agility, Ballistic Skill, Defence, Fellowship, Finesse
  • Role Bonus — Move and Shoot: Once per round, after performing a Move action, the Desperado may make a single Standard Attack with a Pistol weapon as a Free Action. This attack suffers a −10 penalty to Ballistic Skill.

Hierophant

Preachers, demagogues, and spiritual leaders. The Hierophant commands through faith and force of personality.

  • Aptitudes: Fellowship, Offence, Social, Toughness, Willpower
  • Role Bonus — Sway the Masses: Once per session, the Hierophant may make a Challenging (+0) Charm or Intimidate test to inspire or terrify a crowd. On success, the crowd acts favourably (or fearfully) for 1d5 rounds plus the Hierophant's Fellowship bonus.

Mystic

Psykers, seers, and those who channel the dangerous power of the Warp. The Mystic wields abilities that terrify friend and foe alike.

  • Aptitudes: Defence, Intelligence, Knowledge, Perception, Willpower
  • Role Bonus — Stare into the Warp: The Mystic may spend a Fate point to automatically succeed on a Focus Power test with a number of degrees of success equal to their Willpower bonus. They still roll for Psychic Phenomena as normal.

Sage

Scholars, analysts, and data-hunters. The Sage uncovers the truth through research, deduction, and accumulated knowledge.

  • Aptitudes: Intelligence, Knowledge, Perception, Tech, Willpower
  • Role Bonus — Quest for Knowledge: Once per session, the Sage may spend a Fate point to automatically succeed on a Logic or any Lore test with a number of degrees of success equal to their Intelligence bonus.

Seeker

Investigators, trackers, and interrogators. The Seeker finds what others would keep hidden.

  • Aptitudes: Fellowship, Intelligence, Perception, Social, Tech
  • Role Bonus — Nothing Escapes My Sight: The Seeker may spend a Fate point to automatically succeed on an Awareness or Inquiry test with a number of degrees of success equal to their Perception bonus.

Warrior

Soldiers, enforcers, and front-line combatants. The Warrior holds the line when the shooting starts.

  • Aptitudes: Ballistic Skill or Weapon Skill, Defence, Fieldcraft, Offence, Toughness
  • Role Bonus — Unrelenting Devastation: After making a successful attack, the Warrior may spend a Fate point to inflict additional damage equal to their degrees of success on the attack roll to the first hit of that attack. This additional damage ignores armour and Toughness bonus.

Spending Experience Points

Every starting character receives 1,000 XP to spend during character creation. This XP can be spent on improving characteristics, purchasing skills, or acquiring talents.

The Aptitude System

Dark Heresy 2nd Edition uses an aptitude-based advancement system. Your character accumulates aptitudes from their homeworld (1), background (1), and role (5), plus the universal aptitude General that all characters possess. The total is typically 8 aptitudes.

Every advance (characteristic increase, skill, or talent) is associated with two aptitudes. The cost of the advance depends on how many of those aptitudes your character possesses:

  • Both aptitudes match — Cheapest cost tier.
  • One aptitude matches — Middle cost tier.
  • No aptitudes match — Most expensive cost tier.

Characteristic Advances

Characteristics can be improved in increments of +5, up to four times (Simple, Intermediate, Trained, Proficient, Expert). Each tier costs more than the last.

Advance2 Aptitudes1 Aptitude0 Aptitudes
Simple (+5)100 XP250 XP500 XP
Intermediate (+5)250 XP500 XP750 XP
Trained (+5)500 XP750 XP1,000 XP
Proficient (+5)750 XP1,000 XP1,500 XP
Expert (+5)1,000 XP1,500 XP2,500 XP

Skill Advances

Skills have four levels: Known (+0), Trained (+10), Experienced (+20), and Veteran (+30).

Advance2 Aptitudes1 Aptitude0 Aptitudes
Known (+0)100 XP200 XP300 XP
Trained (+10)200 XP400 XP600 XP
Experienced (+20)300 XP600 XP900 XP
Veteran (+30)400 XP800 XP1,200 XP

Talent Advances

Tier2 Aptitudes1 Aptitude0 Aptitudes
Tier 1200 XP300 XP600 XP
Tier 2300 XP450 XP900 XP
Tier 3400 XP600 XP1,200 XP

Divination

As a final step in this phase, roll 1d100 on the Divination table. This represents an Imperial prophecy, omen, or fate that hangs over your character. Each result grants a permanent mechanical bonus (or occasionally a trade-off) that lasts for the character's entire career.

Divinations range from combat bonuses to social advantages to strange blessings. The result is permanent and cannot be changed — the Emperor's will is not negotiable.

Consult the GM for the Divination table, as results vary by campaign.

Starting Equipment

Your starting equipment is primarily determined by your background (see Step 3). Each background lists specific weapons, armour, and gear that your character begins play with. In addition, all characters start with the following:

  • Clothing appropriate to their station
  • An aquila necklace or equivalent symbol of Imperial faith
  • One set of poor-weather gear (cloak, hat, etc.)

Your GM may provide additional equipment based on the specific mission or campaign setup.

Influence

Rather than tracking individual wealth, Dark Heresy 2nd Edition uses the Influence stat. Influence represents your character's ability to acquire goods, call in favours, and leverage their connections.

Your starting Influence is determined by your homeworld and background modifiers. During play, Influence is tested whenever you attempt to acquire equipment, secure passage, or request aid from Imperial institutions.

Influence can rise or fall based on your actions — completing missions successfully may increase it, while failure, scandal, or association with heresy will erode it.

Derived Statistics

Once all choices are made and XP is spent, calculate your derived statistics.

Wounds

Your starting wounds are determined by your homeworld. Each homeworld lists a base value plus a 1d5 roll:

  • Feral World: 9 + 1d5
  • Forge World: 8 + 1d5
  • Highborn: 9 + 1d5
  • Hive World: 8 + 1d5
  • Shrine World: 8 + 1d5
  • Voidborn: 7 + 1d5

Fate Points

Your Fate Threshold is set by your homeworld. Roll 1d10 — if the result is equal to or greater than the Emperor's Blessing number listed for your homeworld, you gain one additional fate point above your threshold.

Fate points can be spent to re-roll a failed test, gain a bonus, or recover from a critical injury. Once spent, they regenerate at the start of each session. A fate point can also be permanently burned to survive certain death — but once burned, it is gone forever.

Movement

Your movement rates are derived from your Agility Bonus (AgB):

  • Half Move: AgB metres
  • Full Move: AgB × 2 metres
  • Charge: AgB × 3 metres
  • Run: AgB × 6 metres

Finishing Touches

With all mechanics in place, take a moment to flesh out your character:

  • Name: Choose a name appropriate to your homeworld and station.
  • Appearance: Describe your character's physical appearance, notable scars, augmetics, or distinguishing marks.
  • Personality: Consider how your homeworld, background, and role have shaped your character's demeanour.
  • Connections: Discuss with your GM and fellow players how your characters might know each other or how you were recruited by the Inquisition.

Your character is now ready to serve the Emperor. The Inquisition has need of you, Acolyte. Do not fail.
